topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, the stark lines of the genetic report mocking me. My hands, shaking slightly, traced the names listed under 'Biological Parent.' A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a familiar, unwelcome clench. I reread the data, each percentage point a tiny, sharp shard of something unpleasant. It wasn't a revelation; it was an indictment. I pushed the paper away with unnecessary force, the sound loud in the silence of the apartment. I stared out the window at the indifferent city lights, feeling a deep, hollow ache spread through my chest. Every memory of warmth, every moment of belonging, suddenly felt tainted, a performance I hadn't been privy to. I didn't cry; I just stood there, rigid, letting the sheer unfairness of it settle over me like a heavy shroud.

[story 2]

"So, this is it," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. The results confirmed what I already suspected: I wasn't an accident; I was a calculated placement. I slammed my laptop shut, the plastic protesting the sudden violence. My jaw was tight enough to crack enamel. I walked to the bathroom mirror, not to check my appearance, but to confront the stranger staring back—a stranger whose origins were suddenly mapped out in cold, undeniable science. A slow, deliberate movement brought my hand up, pressing hard against my temples. The pressure was a physical manifestation of the churning resentment inside. I looked at my reflection, seeing only a vessel, a carefully curated product, and a wave of profound dissatisfaction washed over me. I turned away, walking toward the bedroom, every step heavy with unspoken accusations.

[story 3]

The confirmation email sat glowing on my screen, a beacon of unwelcome truth. I picked up the device, my fingers hovering over the 'download' button before pulling back, a sharp intake of breath catching in my throat. The sheer audacity of it—that my very blueprint had been cataloged and assigned. I paced the length of the living room, the floorboards groaning beneath my agitated stride. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, as I tried to process the implications. There was no anger yet, just a profound sense of being fundamentally misplaced, like a misplaced puzzle piece that was never meant to fit. I stopped abruptly by the bookshelf, running a hand over the spines of old books, feeling a deep, corrosive sense of injustice bloom in my core. I felt utterly untethered, a specimen examined and filed away.
